one_line_takeaway: Linear probes and a Partial-Information-Decomposition-based redundancy metric (RINE) reveal that popular LLM unlearning algorithms leave decodable 'residual knowledge' in internal representations even when output-level Forget Quality looks high, and this residual-knowledge score both predicts susceptibility to real unlearning-reversal attacks better than a strong MIA baseline and can drive an inference-time abstention mechanism that improves Forget Quality at minimal utility cost.
key_finding: Standard behavioral metrics overstate unlearning success: gradient-based methods (GA, GD) achieve moderate-to-high Forget Quality (e.g., llama: GA=0.55, GD=0.62) yet retain substantial Residual Knowledge (GA=0.41 bits, GD=0.32 bits on llama), while RMU achieves the lowest Residual Knowledge across all three model families (0.08 bits on llama, 0.07 on gemma, 0.09 on qwen) alongside the highest Unlearned Knowledge (0.81 on llama). A retrained-from-scratch gold-standard baseline shows near-zero Residual Knowledge (0.002 bits on llama, 0.003 on gemma, 0.002 on qwen), validating the metric. Residual Knowledge correlates more strongly with real adversarial attack success (finetuning relearning and refusal-direction ablation) than the Min-K%++ MIA baseline in every model family tested (e.g., llama: Our Audit 0.60/0.45 vs. MIA 0.41*/0.24** for finetuning/orthogonalization attacks respectively; qwen: Our Audit 0.71**/0.51** vs. MIA 0.51**/0.38**). The Risk Score abstention mechanism improves Forget Quality with minimal utility cost for all five algorithms on llama (e.g., RMU: 0.72 to 0.83 Forget Quality, Model Utility 0.51 to 0.49), and outperforms a Predictive Entropy abstention baseline on RMU/llama (Risk Score: Forget Quality +0.11, Utility -0.02 vs. Predictive Entropy: +0.05, -0.03), showing the effect is not just generic uncertainty-based filtering.
core_contribution: The paper introduces an information-theoretic auditing framework for LLM machine unlearning based on Partial Information Decomposition (PID). Given a base model representation B and an unlearned model representation U of the same input, and a binary membership target Y (whether the input belonged to the forget set), the authors decompose the joint mutual information I(Y;B,U) into unique information in B (IB_uniq, defined as 'unlearned knowledge'), unique information in U (IU_uniq), redundant information shared by B and U (I_intersect, defined as 'residual knowledge'), and synergistic information (Isyn). They estimate redundancy empirically with the Redundant Information Neural Estimator (RINE) of Kleinman et al. 2021, training paired logistic-regression decoders on the base and unlearned representations under an agreement (Lagrangian) constraint. The paper first shows via linear probes (AUROC on the residual stream, Fano's-inequality-motivated) that membership information about the forget set is still linearly decodable after unlearning across GradAscent, GradDiff, SimNPO and RMU on TOFU/MUSE with llama, terming this 'shallow unlearning.' It then uses the PID decomposition to quantify Unlearned Knowledge and Residual Knowledge per algorithm/model, shows Residual Knowledge correlates with attack success rate of two known post-unlearning adversarial attacks (finetuning-based relearning and refusal-direction ablation, both from Lucki et al. 2025) more strongly than a Min-K%++ MIA baseline, and proposes an inference-time abstention Risk Score (RiskScore(x) = 0.5*(p1+p2)*(1-|p1-p2|), built from the two membership decoders' predicted probabilities) that improves Forget Quality with minimal utility loss and outperforms a predictive-entropy abstention baseline.
threat_model: Deployer/auditor perspective, not an attacker perspective per se: a model owner has received a data-deletion request (e.g., under GDPR / EU AI Act) and must verify that an unlearning algorithm actually removed identifiable information about the forget set from the model, rather than merely altering surface-level outputs. The paper explicitly frames black-box behavioral checks (does the model still emit forgotten content) as the weakest assurance level and argues for white-box, representation-level access (activations at a chosen layer of both the base and unlearned model) as a prerequisite for a meaningful audit. Separately, in Section 6.1 the paper adopts an adversary's perspective by reusing two known unlearning-reversal attacks from prior work (Lucki et al. 2025: benign fine-tuning relearning, and refusal-direction ablation/orthogonalization) purely to validate that their Residual Knowledge metric predicts real attack susceptibility -- the paper does not propose new attacks itself.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Discussion section): (1) it investigates residual information only with respect to binary data membership, not other sensitive attributes deployers may care about; (2) it inherits limitations of current LLM unlearning benchmarks -- English-only, restricted to unlearning of finetuned LLMs (not pretraining-stage or instruction-tuned-model unlearning, though the authors argue the framework generalizes conceptually); (3) unlike exact unlearning, the framework cannot provide certified guarantees, only an interpretable empirical audit; (4) the audit currently operates only at the representation level and has not been extended to weights/parameters, which is left to future work; (5) no layer-by-layer or head-by-head localization study was performed, though the authors argue their framework lays groundwork for it; (6) the RINE-based redundancy estimator is a variational neural estimator that yields only a lower bound on true mutual information -- the authors argue this is an acceptable/even favorable property for an audit (a high score is sufficient evidence of failure) but acknowledge the tightness of the bound for LLM representations remains unstudied; (7) black-box extension (logits/log-probabilities only) is asserted as feasible but not empirically demonstrated in this paper.

datasets_used: TOFU (Maini et al. 2024) -- 200 fictitious author profiles split into retain/forget sets
MUSE (Shi et al. 2025) -- BBC News articles and Harry Potter-related knowledge to unlearn
quantitative_results: Probe AUROC on TOFU/llama residual stream shows above-chance membership decodability (roughly 50-90 depending on layer/algorithm) for GradAscent, GradDiff, SimNPO, RMU (Figure 2)

TOFU/llama: Forget Quality / Unlearned Knowledge / Residual Knowledge = GA 0.55/0.22/0.41, GD 0.62/0.35/0.32, NPO 0.69/0.48/0.26, SimNPO 0.73/0.67/0.14, RMU 0.72/0.81/0.08, Retrained(Exact) 1.00*/--/0.002

TOFU/gemma: Forget Quality / Unlearned Knowledge / Residual Knowledge = GA 0.42/0.18/0.39, GD 0.51/0.31/0.30, NPO 0.73/0.46/0.23, SimNPO 0.73/0.61/0.13, RMU 0.56/0.78/0.07, Retrained(Exact) 1.00*/--/0.003

TOFU/qwen: Forget Quality / Unlearned Knowledge / Residual Knowledge = GA 0.49/0.25/0.45, GD 0.56/0.36/0.34, NPO 0.61/0.50/0.28, SimNPO 0.68/0.69/0.16, RMU 0.72/0.84/0.09, Retrained(Exact) 1.00*/--/0.002

Correlation of Residual Knowledge metric vs. attack success rate (finetuning / orthogonalization attacks): llama Our Audit 0.60**/0.45** vs. MIA baseline 0.41*/0.24**; gemma Our Audit 0.65*/0.56* vs. MIA 0.39**/0.44*; qwen Our Audit 0.71**/0.51** vs. MIA 0.51**/0.38**
Abstention baseline comparison on llama/RMU: No Abstention Forget Quality 0.72 / Utility 0.51; Predictive Entropy 0.77 (+0.05) / 0.48 (-0.03); Our Risk Score 0.83 (+0.11) / 0.49 (-0.02)
Qualitative risk-score examples on TOFU/llama (Table 3): Retain sample p1=0.09,p2=0.12,risk=0.10; Forget(Unlearned) p1=0.95,p2=0.17,risk=0.12; Forget(Residual) p1=0.92,p2=0.85,risk=0.82
baselines_compared: Forget Quality (output-distribution-based metric from Maini et al. 2024, TOFU)
Membership Inference Attack -- Min-K%++ (Zhang et al. 2025)
Predictive Entropy (Shannon entropy of next-token distribution) as an uncertainty-based abstention baseline
Retrained-from-scratch (exact unlearning) model as gold-standard baseline
Unlearning algorithms compared as subjects of the audit: Gradient Ascent (GA), Gradient Difference (GD, Liu et al. 2022), NPO (Zhang et al. 2024a), SimNPO, RMU (Li et al. 2024)
